sublevels.title: BOOLEANS & CONDITIONS
sublevels.subtitle: True or False — the foundation of logic

teaching.title: What is a Condition?
teaching.analogy: A condition is a yes/no question. 'Is the door open?' — the answer is either True or False. Computers make EVERY decision this way.
teaching.content: A condition is any expression that evaluates to True or False. Comparison operators (>, <, ==, !=) create conditions. Python uses these to decide what code to run.

examples.title: Conditions in Action
examples.explanation: Each line evaluates to True or False.
examples.code:
print(5 > 3)     # True
print(10 < 2)    # False
print(5 == 5)    # True
print(5 != 5)    # False

teaching.funFact: The == operator checks equality. The = operator assigns values. Mixing them up is one of the most common bugs! 🐛

teaching.title: Comparison Operators
teaching.analogy: Comparison operators are like a judge weighing two things on a scale and announcing the verdict: True or False.
teaching.content: Python has 6 comparison operators: > (greater), < (less), >= (greater or equal), <= (less or equal), == (equals), != (not equals). They always return True or False.

examples.title: All 6 Operators
examples.code:
print(10 > 5)    # True
print(3 < 1)     # False
print(5 >= 5)    # True
print(4 <= 3)    # False
print(7 == 7)    # True
print(8 != 8)    # False

teaching.funFact: You can even compare strings! 'apple' < 'banana' is True because 'a' comes before 'b' alphabetically. 🍎

mcq.q: What does 10 > 5 evaluate to?
mcq.explanation: 10 is greater than 5, so the expression is True.

mcq.q: What does 3 == '3' evaluate to in Python?
mcq.explanation: 3 (int) and '3' (str) are different types, so == returns False.

mcq.q: Which operator means 'not equal to'?
mcq.explanation: != is the 'not equal to' operator. It returns True if values are different.

mcq.q: What does 5 >= 5 return?
mcq.explanation: 5 is equal to 5, and >= means 'greater than OR equal'. So it's True.

mcq.q: What is the difference between = and ==?
mcq.explanation: = is assignment (store a value). == is comparison (check if equal).

coding.title: True or False?
coding.objective: What does 7 > 10 evaluate to? Type True or False.

coding.title: Equality Check
coding.hint: Both strings are exactly the same.

coding.title: Not Equal
coding.objective: What operator checks if two values are NOT the same? Type the operator.
coding.starterCode: # x ___ y  → True if x is not equal to y
coding.hint: It's an exclamation mark followed by equals.

debugging.hint: = assigns. == compares. In an if statement, you need ==.
= is assignment. To check equality, use ==.

prediction.code:
x = 10
y = 20
print(x > y)
print(x < y)

a = 5
b = 5
print(a == b)
print(a != b)
prediction.explanation: 5 == 5 is True. 5 != 5 is False (they ARE equal).

teaching.title: The if Statement
teaching.analogy: An if statement is like a traffic light. If the light is green (True), you GO (run the code). If red (False), you STOP (skip it).
teaching.content: The if statement checks a condition. If it's True, the indented code below runs. If False, Python skips it entirely. The colon (:) and indentation (4 spaces) are required!

examples.title: Basic if
examples.explanation: The indented line only runs if the condition is True.
examples.code:
age = 15
if age >= 13:
    print('Welcome, teen!')
# Output: Welcome, teen!

teaching.funFact: Python uses INDENTATION (spaces) to define code blocks. Most other languages use curly braces {}. Python's way is cleaner! ✨

teaching.title: if-else — Two Paths
teaching.analogy: if-else is a fork in the road. If the condition is True, go LEFT. If False, go RIGHT. You ALWAYS take one path — never both, never neither.
teaching.content: else provides an alternative path when the if condition is False. Exactly ONE of the two blocks will always execute — guaranteed.

examples.title: if-else Fork
examples.explanation: One block ALWAYS runs. Never both, never neither.

teaching.funFact: You can chain conditions with elif (else if) for multiple paths: if → elif → elif → else! 🔗

teaching.title: elif — Multiple Paths
teaching.analogy: elif is like a restaurant menu with multiple options. Python checks each one from top to bottom and picks the FIRST True match.
teaching.content: elif (else if) lets you check multiple conditions in order. Python tries each from top to bottom and runs the FIRST one that's True. If none are True, else runs.

examples.title: Grade Calculator
examples.explanation: Python checks top-down, picks first True match.

teaching.funFact: You can have as many elif blocks as you want, but only ONE if and ONE else per chain! 📏

mcq.q: What character MUST come at the end of an if line?
mcq.explanation: Every if, elif, and else line must end with a colon (:).

mcq.q: How does Python know which code belongs to an if block?
mcq.explanation: Python uses indentation (4 spaces or 1 tab) to define code blocks.

mcq.q: In if-else, how many blocks run?
mcq.explanation: Exactly one block runs — either the if block (True) or the else block (False).

mcq.q: What does elif stand for?
mcq.explanation: elif = else if. It provides additional conditions to check.

mcq.q: In if→elif→else, Python picks the ___ True match.
mcq.explanation: Python checks from top to bottom and runs the FIRST True condition.

coding.title: Write an if
coding.objective: What keyword starts a decision in Python? Type the keyword.
coding.hint: It's a 2-letter word that means 'in case of'.

coding.title: What Prints?
coding.objective: x = 3. if x > 5: print('big'). else: print('small'). What prints?

coding.title: Grade Logic
coding.objective: marks = 85. After if >=90: 'A', elif >=70: 'B', elif >=50: 'C', else: 'F'. What grade?
coding.hint: 85 >= 90? No. 85 >= 70? Yes! B is the first True match.

options: Missing colon (:) at the end of the if line
debugging.answer: Missing colon (:) at the end of the if line
debugging.hint: Every if statement must end with a colon: if x > 5:

options: Missing indentation — print must be 4 spaces inside the if block
debugging.answer: Missing indentation — print must be 4 spaces inside the if block
debugging.hint: Code inside an if block MUST be indented (4 spaces).

prediction.explanation: x=10. 10>100? No. 10>5? Yes! 'B' is printed. Even though else exists, Python already found a match.
These are TWO separate if statements (not if-elif). Both conditions are True, so BOTH print.

teaching.title: AND — Both Must Be True
teaching.analogy: AND is like a team challenge: BOTH conditions must be True to win. If even one is False, the whole thing is False.
teaching.content: The 'and' operator combines two conditions. The result is True ONLY if BOTH sides are True. If either side is False, the whole expression is False.

examples.title: AND Gate
examples.code:
age = 15
has_ticket = True
if age >= 13 and has_ticket:
    print('Enter the movie!')
# Both are True → prints!

teaching.funFact: In circuit logic, AND gates need BOTH inputs ON to produce output. Same idea! ⚡

teaching.title: OR — At Least One True
teaching.analogy: OR is like having two keys to the same door. If EITHER key works, the door opens. Only fails if NEITHER works.
teaching.content: The 'or' operator returns True if AT LEAST one condition is True. It only returns False when BOTH sides are False.

examples.title: OR Gate
examples.code:
is_admin = False
is_owner = True
if is_admin or is_owner:
    print('Access granted!')
# One is True → prints!

teaching.funFact: Python uses short-circuit evaluation: if the first condition of 'or' is True, it doesn't even check the second! 🏎️

teaching.title: NOT — Flip the Boolean
teaching.analogy: NOT is like a mirror: it flips the truth. True becomes False. False becomes True. It reverses any boolean.
teaching.content: The 'not' operator flips a boolean value. not True → False. not False → True. Use it to check 'if something is NOT the case'.

examples.title: NOT — The Flip
examples.code:
is_blocked = False
if not is_blocked:
    print('You may pass!')
# not False = True → prints!

teaching.funFact: Double negation: not not True = True. It cancels out, like multiplying by -1 twice! 🔁

mcq.q: True and False evaluates to?
mcq.explanation: AND requires BOTH to be True. Since one is False, result is False.

mcq.q: True or False evaluates to?
mcq.explanation: OR only needs ONE True. Since one is True, result is True.

mcq.q: not True evaluates to?
mcq.explanation: not flips the boolean: not True = False.

mcq.q: False and False evaluates to?
mcq.explanation: AND needs both True. Both are False → result is False.

mcq.q: False or False evaluates to?
mcq.explanation: OR needs at least one True. Both are False → result is False.

coding.title: AND Truth
coding.objective: What does True and True evaluate to?

coding.title: OR Truth
coding.objective: What does False or True evaluate to?
coding.hint: OR: At least one True → True.

coding.title: NOT Flip
coding.objective: What does not False evaluate to?

debugging.hint: Python logical operators are all lowercase: and, or, not.
Python keywords are all lowercase. not, not NOT.

prediction.explanation: 10>5 is True. 3<5 is True. True and True = True.

prediction.code:
a = True
b = False
print(not a or b)
prediction.explanation: not True = False. False or False = False.

teaching.title: Nested if Statements
teaching.analogy: Nested ifs are like Russian dolls — an if inside another if. First you check the outer condition, and only if that's True, you check the inner one.
teaching.content: You can put an if statement INSIDE another if statement. The inner if only runs when the outer if is True. Each level needs more indentation (4 more spaces).

examples.title: Nested Check
examples.explanation: Inner check only happens if outer is True.

teaching.funFact: Most experienced programmers try to AVOID deep nesting. If you're 4+ levels deep, there's usually a cleaner way! 🧹

teaching.title: Real-World Decision Patterns
teaching.analogy: Every app you use is full of if statements: 'If password correct, log in. If battery low, show warning. If message unread, show badge.'
teaching.content: Decision logic is everywhere: login systems, game logic, notifications, calculators, traffic lights. Learning if/else/elif is learning to think like software.

examples.title: Login Check
examples.explanation: Real apps use exactly this pattern.

teaching.funFact: The average iPhone has over 100 MILLION lines of code, most of which are if-else decisions! 📱

mcq.q: In nested if, the inner if runs when?
options: Only when the outer if is True
Only when the outer if is False
mcq.explanation: Inner if only executes when the outer condition is True.

mcq.q: How many extra spaces does a nested if need?
mcq.explanation: Each nesting level adds 4 more spaces of indentation.

mcq.q: What is the main use of if-else in real apps?
mcq.explanation: if-else is the fundamental decision-making tool in all software.

mcq.q: Can you use 'and' inside an if condition?
mcq.explanation: You can use and, or, not inside any if/elif condition to combine checks.

coding.title: Nested Logic
coding.objective:
In this code, what prints?
age=20, has_ticket=False
if age>=18: if has_ticket: 'Enjoy' else: 'Buy ticket' else: 'Too young'
coding.hint: 20>=18 is True (enter outer). has_ticket is False (else inner).

coding.title: Password Check
coding.objective:
User types 'abc'. Password is 'secret'. What prints?
if password == 'secret': 'Login!' else: 'Wrong!'

options: The inner 'if has_ticket' needs to be indented 4 spaces inside the outer if
debugging.answer: The inner 'if has_ticket' needs to be indented 4 spaces inside the outer if
debugging.hint: Nested if must be indented inside its parent if block.

options: ' else' has a leading space — else must align with if
debugging.answer: ' else' has a leading space — else must align with if
debugging.hint: else must be at the SAME indentation level as its matching if.

prediction.explanation: 15>10 is True (enter outer). 15>20 is False (inner else). Prints 'B'.
5>3 is True AND 10<20 is True. True and True = True → 'YES'.

teaching.title: ⚠️ BOSS — THE DECISION ENGINE
teaching.analogy: The Decision Engine controls all crossroads. It will test your mastery of conditions, if/elif/else, logical operators, and nested decisions!
teaching.content: THE DECISION ENGINE has absorbed all of Level 2. Every path splits into True/False. Prove you can navigate the logic maze!

examples.title: What you've mastered
examples.explanation: Everything from Level 2 is in play!
examples.code:
✅ Conditions & comparisons
✅ if / elif / else
✅ and, or, not
✅ Nested if statements

teaching.funFact: The most complex software in the world (like self-driving cars) is just billions of if-else decisions running per second! 🚗

mcq.explanation: 7>10? No. 7>5? Yes! 'B' prints. Python stops at the FIRST True match.

mcq.q: True and True or False?
mcq.explanation: (True and True) = True. True or False = True.

mcq.q: What runs if ALL conditions in an if/elif chain are False?
mcq.explanation: else is the fallback — it runs when no if or elif condition is True.

mcq.q: not (5 > 3) evaluates to?
mcq.explanation: 5>3 is True. not True = False.

mcq.q: How many levels of nesting is considered 'too deep' by most programmers?
mcq.explanation: More than 3-4 levels deep usually means the code should be refactored for clarity.

coding.title: Boss: Chain Logic
coding.objective: score=85. if >=90:'A' elif >=80:'B' elif >=70:'C' else:'F'. What grade?

coding.title: Boss: Combined Check
coding.objective: What does (False or True) and (not False) evaluate to?
coding.starterCode:
print((False or True) and (not False))
# Output: ___
coding.hint: (False or True)=True. (not False)=True. True and True = True.

debugging.description:
🚨 BOSS BUG:

x = 10
if x > 5 or < 20:
    print('Range OK')
options: Must repeat x: if x > 5 or x < 20
debugging.answer: Must repeat x: if x > 5 or x < 20
debugging.hint: Each side of 'or' needs a full condition. Can't shorthand '< 20' alone.

prediction.explanation: Three SEPARATE if statements. 5>3→A. 5>4→B. 5>5→False (not >). Output: A B.
